name: Bioinformatics
description:
An introduction to **computational biology** — using computers to analyse biological data.

> This quiz uses `question_limit: 4`, so each session randomly selects 4 out of 5 questions.

Topics covered:
- Sequence formats (FASTA, FASTQ, BAM)
- Sequence alignment
- Genome assembly
- Tools like BLAST

questions.text: What does the **FASTA** file format store?

choices.text: 3D protein structures and atomic coordinates (PDB format)
Biological sequences such as DNA or proteins
Gene expression levels measured by microarray or RNA-seq
Chromosome karyotype images and banding patterns

questions.explanation:
**FASTA** is one of the most common formats for storing biological sequences.

A FASTA file looks like this:

```
>sequence_name description
ATGCATGCATGCATGCATGC
ATGCATGCATGCATGC
```

- Lines starting with `>` are **headers** (sequence name/description)
- The following lines are the **sequence** itself (DNA letters A, T, G, C or amino acids)

FASTA can store:
- DNA sequences
- RNA sequences
- Protein (amino acid) sequences

questions.text: Which bioinformatics tool is commonly used to **search a sequence database** for similar sequences?
questions.explanation:
**BLAST** (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool) is used to find sequences in databases that are similar to a query sequence.

How it works:

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q["Query sequence"] --> BLAST["BLAST search"]
    DB[("Sequence database\ne.g. NCBI")] --> BLAST
    BLAST --> R["Matching sequences + scores"]
```

BLAST gives each match an **E-value** — a lower E-value means a more significant match.

**SAMtools** works with alignment files (SAM/BAM), not database searches.

questions.text: Which of the following are **common bioinformatics file formats** used to store sequencing data? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation:
Common bioinformatics formats:

| Format | Contains | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| **FASTA** | Sequences only | Reference genomes, protein databases |
| **FASTQ** | Sequences + quality scores | Raw sequencing reads |
| **BAM** | Aligned reads (binary) | Read alignment to a reference |
| **VCF** | Variants | SNPs, indels |

**DOCX** is a Microsoft Word format — not used in bioinformatics!

questions.text:
In a **sequence alignment**, what does a gap symbol (`-`) represent?

Example alignment:
```
Sequence A:  ATG-CTAGC
Sequence B:  ATGGCTA-C
```
questions.explanation:
In a sequence alignment, `-` is a **gap** — it represents an **insertion or deletion (indel)**.

When two sequences are aligned, gaps are introduced to make similar regions line up:

```
Sequence A:  ATG-CTAGC   ← gap = a base was deleted (or inserted in B)
Sequence B:  ATGGCTA-C   ← gap = a base was deleted (or inserted in A)
             |||.|||.|
```

- A gap in sequence A means B has an **insertion** at that position (or A has a deletion)
- Gaps are necessary to produce the best possible alignment

questions.text: In your own words, what is **genome assembly**? Why is it needed?
questions.explanation:
**Genome assembly** is the process of reconstructing a complete genome sequence from many short sequencing reads.

Why it's needed: DNA sequencers cannot read an entire genome in one go — they produce millions of short fragments (reads). Assembly software pieces them together like a puzzle:

```mermaid
graph LR
    Reads["Short reads\n(100-300 bp each)"] --> Assembler["Assembly software"]
    Assembler --> Contigs["Contigs (longer sequences)"]
    Contigs --> Genome["Complete genome"]
```

**Key concepts:**
- **Read** — a short sequenced fragment
- **Contig** — a contiguous assembled sequence
- **Scaffold** — contigs ordered and oriented using additional information
